text: A categorical variable places cases into groups or labels. A quantitative variable records numerical measurements where arithmetic such as averaging is meaningful. Bar charts are commonly used for categorical variables, while histograms are commonly used for quantitative variables.

A resistant measure is not strongly affected by extreme values. The median is resistant because it depends on the middle position after sorting the data. The mean is not resistant because very large or very small observations pull it toward the tail of a skewed distribution.

A z-score tells how many standard deviations an observation is above or below the mean. In a normal distribution, z-scores can be used with the standard normal curve to find approximate probabilities and percentiles.

A regression line summarizes a linear relationship between an explanatory variable and a response variable. A residual is observed value minus predicted value. Residual plots help check whether a straight-line model is reasonable.

A sample is biased when the sampling method systematically favors some outcomes over others. Random sampling helps reduce selection bias because each member of the population has a known chance to be selected.

In an experiment, random assignment assigns subjects to treatments by chance. Random assignment helps balance lurking variables across treatment groups, which supports cause-and-effect conclusions when the study is well designed.

Two events are independent when knowing that one event occurred does not change the probability of the other event. If events are independent, the probability of both events occurring is the product of their probabilities.

A sampling distribution describes how a statistic varies from sample to sample. The central limit theorem says that the sampling distribution of the sample mean becomes approximately normal for large enough samples under broad conditions.

A binomial setting has a fixed number of trials, two possible outcomes on each trial, the same probability of success on each trial, and independent trials. The count of successes is the binomial random variable.

A confidence interval estimates an unknown population parameter using a statistic plus or minus a margin of error. A larger sample size usually reduces the margin of error when other conditions stay the same.

A p-value is the probability, assuming the null hypothesis is true, of getting a result at least as extreme as the observed result. Small p-values provide stronger evidence against the null hypothesis.

The symbol p_hat represents the sample proportion. The symbol p_0 represents a hypothesized population proportion used in a one-proportion hypothesis test. The standard error describes typical sample-to-sample variation in a statistic.
